title: Why did they call you that?
subtitle: Reading and Workbook practice: names and vowel sounds

blocks.title: Reading · Naming customs around the world
blocks.text: Read the article, then complete exercises 3c and 3d.

sections.heading: Korea
sections.text: Names in Korea are written with the surname first, and the first name is written as two parts. So, if Yeon Seok has the surname Lee, his name is written Lee Yeon Seok. Two-part first names are never shortened; that is, Lee Yeon Seok will always be called Yeon Seok, not Yeon.

sections.heading: Russia
sections.text: Russian names have three parts: a first name, a patronymic (a middle name based on the father’s first name), and the father’s surname. If Viktor Aleksandrovich Rakhmaninov has two children, his son’s name might be Mikhail Viktorevich Rakhmaninov and his daughter’s name might be Svetlana Viktorovna Rakhmaninova (the ‘a’ at the end of all three names shows that she is female).

sections.heading: Spanish-speaking countries
sections.text: In most Spanish-speaking countries, people have a first name (sometimes in two parts, e.g. Maria José, Juan Carlos) and two surnames, their father’s and their mother’s, for example, Maria José López Ramírez. In Spain, traditionally the father’s surname goes first, followed by the mother’s, but nowadays the order can be reversed. However, both surnames are always used and on any form, people will be asked for their surnames, not their surname.

sections.heading: India
sections.text: India has many religions and languages which influence the naming customs. Hindu first names are usually based on the position of the planets at the date and time of birth, but the names are often shortened by family and friends. For example, a brother and sister may have the formal names Aditya and Aarushi, but family and friends may call them Adi and Ashi for short.

sections.heading: China
sections.text: Chinese names are made up of three ‘characters’: a one-character surname followed by a two-character first name, e.g. Li Xiu Ying. A child’s official name is used for their birth certificate and for school, but Chinese children often have a different name that is used among friends and classmates.

sections.heading: Afghanistan
sections.text: Afghan names traditionally consist of only a first name. Male first names are usually Arabic double names, e.g. Abdul Ahad, and women are generally given Persian names, e.g. Jasmine. Surnames are chosen only when needed. Commonly, this is when people have contact with the Western world. The surname may be related to the tribe the person comes from, their place of birth, or their profession, e.g. ‘Doctor’. This may result in people within the same family having different surnames.

sections.heading: The UK
sections.text: Since the 15th century, British women have taken their husband’s surname when they get married, so when Sophie Jones marries Peter Elliot, she becomes Mrs Sophie Elliot. However, 14% of married women now choose to keep their maiden name. If they do so, couples sometimes then combine their surnames for their children, so if Sophie decided to keep the surname Jones, their children might have the surname Jones-Elliot or Elliot-Jones.

blocks.title: 3c. Read the article again. In which country or countries…?
blocks.instructions: Choose all the correct answers. Some questions have more than one answer.

items.prompt: does the surname come before the first name?
do people have more than one surname?
do people have a middle name connected to their father’s name?
do some people stop using the surname they were born with?
are people given names depending on when they were born?

blocks.title: 3d. Write about naming customs in your country
blocks.instructions: Answer in English. This task is saved and sent to the teacher, but it is not graded automatically.

items.prompt: What is the naming custom in your country? Has it changed over the years? Do you think it ought to change?

blocks.title: Exercise 1 · Vocabulary: names
blocks.text: Complete parts a, b, and c.

blocks.title: 1a. Match the sentence halves

items.prompt: Her name’s Katheryn, but she’s called…
options: Peter Gene Hernandez to Bruno Mars.
Peter Gene Hernandez to Bruno Mars.

items.prompt: The singer changed his name from…
options: Peter Gene Hernandez to Bruno Mars.
items.explanation: Peter Gene Hernandez to Bruno Mars.

items.prompt: Robert Downey Junior is named after…
options: Peter Gene Hernandez to Bruno Mars.

items.prompt: Ruth Rendell sometimes wrote under the pseudonym…
options: Peter Gene Hernandez to Bruno Mars.
Peter Gene Hernandez to Bruno Mars.

blocks.instructions: Look at the photos and match the sentence halves.

blocks.title: 1b. Complete the definitions with a word from the list

items.prompt: Your ___ is a name that your parents choose for you when you are born.
A ___ is the name between a person’s first name and surname.
A woman’s ___ is her surname before she gets married.
A woman’s ___ is her surname after she gets married.
Your ___ is your whole name, including your first name, any middle names, and your family name.
Your ___ are the first letters of all your names.

blocks.title: 1c. Complete the text with the words from 1a and 1b
blocks.instructions: Write one word in each space. Item 1 is the example.

items.prompt: She wrote six romances under the ___ Mary Westmacott.
Agatha Christie’s ___ name and surname are known worldwide.
Few people know what the rest of the writer’s ___, A.M.C.C., stand for.
Agatha had two ___ names, Mary and Clarissa.
Her ___ name was Agatha Mary Clarissa Christie.
Christie was, in fact, her ___ name — in 1914 she married Archie Christie.
When she married Max Mallowan, she did not ___ her name again.
Her sister Margaret was ___ ‘Madge’ for short.
Madge was ___ after their grandmother’s sister.

blocks.title: Exercise 2 · Pronunciation: vowel sounds
blocks.text: Sort the names by vowel sound, then complete the listening task.

blocks.title: 2a. Write the names in the chart
blocks.instructions: Choose the sound in the stressed syllable. Adele is the example.

blocks.title: 2b. Listen and check. Then listen again and repeat the names.
2c. Listen and complete the sentences
blocks.instructions: Audio 1.2. Type one word or phrase in each gap. Sentence 1 is the example.

items.explanation: Jessica’s sending a message to a friend.
Peter’s seeing his niece this evening.

blocks.title: 2d. Listen again and repeat the sentences.
blocks.text: Use Audio 1.2 and repeat each sentence aloud.
